• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Perfume Collection Analysis

Julissa Dueñas

Goal

As my perfume collection continued to grow, I wanted to take a more intentional, data-driven approach to curating it.

The goal of this project is to streamline my collection, identify a small set of signature scents for each season, and uncover the fragrance notes I naturally gravitate toward based on my wear history.

Data

The two data sets I used for this project are Master_List.csv and Daily_Log.csv. Master_List contains my whole perfume collection. Each perfume has a unique ID, the brand of the fragrance, the name, the type (eau de toilette, body spray, etc.), all of its notes, which size I own, and the price. Daily_Log is my wear history: each row records a date from August 28, 2025 and the ID of the fragrance I wore that day (matching Master_List).

Data preparation

Using the date from ‘Daily_Log’ I derived what season it was. I then split each comma-separated fragrance note into seprate rows and assigned each one its own unique ID so wears can be tied to individual notes

Database Setup

I built a local SQLite database to store the cleaned data and answer my burning questions with SQL. ‘perfumes’ hold my collection, ‘daily_wear_log’ holds wears by date and season, Fragrance notes are normalizes into ‘note’ and ‘perfume_notes’ provides a bridge so one fragrance can map to multiple ntoes.

Analysis: Now the fun part!

How big is my collection? How many full size perfumes do I have versus travel and sample sizes?

size_dist_raw <- dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT size, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM perfumes
GROUP BY size
ORDER BY count DESC
")

total_perfumes <- sum(size_dist_raw$count)

size_dist <- size_dist_raw %>%
  mutate(
    total = total_perfumes,
    share_pct = round(100 * count / total, 1)
  )

In total, I have 192 perfume bottles (yikes). More than 51% of the collection is travel sized bottles.

What are my top 10 perfumes and the notes I am most reaching for?

top10 <- dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT p.name, COUNT(*) as wears
FROM daily_wear_log d
JOIN perfumes p ON d.id = p.id
GROUP BY p.name
ORDER BY wears DESC
LIMIT 10
")
top_notes <- dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT n.note_name, COUNT(*) as wears
FROM daily_wear_log d
JOIN perfume_notes pn ON d.id = pn.id
JOIN notes n ON pn.note_id = n.note_id
GROUP BY n.note_name
ORDER BY wears DESC
LIMIT 10
")

Being as I have 164 days of data, and I have worn a fragrance containing vanilla 107 times, I think it is more than fair to say vanilla earns the top spot

What does this look like by season?

top_by_season<-dbGetQuery(con, "
WITH ranked_perfumes AS (
  SELECT 
    d.season,
    p.brand,
    p.name,
    COUNT(*) AS wear_count
  FROM daily_wear_log d
  JOIN perfumes p ON d.id = p.id
  GROUP BY d.season, p.brand, p.name
)
SELECT *
FROM (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY season
           ORDER BY wear_count DESC
         ) AS rank
  FROM ranked_perfumes
)
WHERE rank <= 5
ORDER BY season, rank;
")
top_notes_by_season <- dbGetQuery(con, "
WITH note_counts AS (
  SELECT 
    d.season,
    n.note_name,
    COUNT(*) AS note_count
  FROM daily_wear_log d
  JOIN perfume_notes pn ON d.id = pn.id
  JOIN notes n ON pn.note_id = n.note_id
  GROUP BY d.season, n.note_name
)
SELECT *
FROM (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY season
           ORDER BY note_count DESC
         ) AS rank
  FROM note_counts
)
WHERE rank <= 10
ORDER BY season, rank;
")

I don’t have too much data from Summer months just yet, but already, I see some floral and fruity notes coming in for the Spring months. I am curious to see how the notes differ based on the seasons.
